access.accessMethod: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog
Date-bounded review of the current BL record, BL digitized-manuscript routes, Biblissima/Arlima discovery pages, and targeted searches
access.format: No whole-object public digital surrogate located
notes: The current BL record exposes no Digitised Content and instead provides onsite/request routes.
Way's printed image of f. 7v is only one page and is separately modeled as partial.
The Biblissima page's generic Mirador/IIIF interface did not expose a manifest or canvas and explicitly reported that no digitized object was available.
A complete-codex photography order may be proposed to BL Digitisation Services, subject to preservation review, fees, and reuse terms; no order was placed.

entities.description: Way's base manuscript; a fifteenth-century parchment copy of the Promptorium at ff. 1r-205v, with adjacent recipes on f. 206r. The current catalog cautions that the copy itself is probably not East Anglian.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 221

physicalDescription.extent: 206 folios, plus modern preliminary and terminal leaves
notes: The Promptorium occupies ff. 1r-205v; medical and household recipes follow on f. 206r.

access.accessMethod: British Library component and parent catalog records

access.format: No whole-object public digital surrogate located
notes: Neither the fragment nor the complete 77-folio composite exposes Digitised Content in the current records.
A request should cover the whole composite physical object, not only ff. 63r-77v, and remains subject to BL feasibility, fees, and terms; no order was placed.

dateStatements.label: Promptorium fragment date
dateStatements.value: second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A disordered fragment of the Promptorium in a composite miscellany; the current component record lists portions of G, H, M, N, O, and R, while Way reports readings from G, H, M, N, and R.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2274, ff. 63r-77v

physicalDescription.extent: Promptorium fragment ff. 63r-77v within a 77-folio composite

dateStatements.label: Manuscript date
dateStatements.value: late fifteenth century; ownership inscription dated 1494

entities.description: Late-fifteenth-century paper schoolbook codex used throughout Way's collation under siglum S and later transcribed directly by A. L. Mayhew.

holding.notes: The institutional page supplies a curator enquiry route but no exact public item record.

entities.label: Winchester Cathedral, MS 15

notes: The codex also contains Latin school texts and retains its original leather-covered oak boards according to Way.

access.accessMethod: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog
Date-bounded review of the current BL record and targeted manuscript-digitization searches
access.format: No whole-object public digital surrogate located
notes: The current record exposes no Digitised Content.
Any complete request should include the entire codex, both Promptorium ff. 1r-83v and adjacent Medulla ff. 84r-174v, plus covers, endleaves, and blanks; no order was placed.

entities.description: Late-fifteenth-century Norfolk paper codex: an imperfect Promptorium at ff. 1r-83v followed by the distinct Medulla grammaticae at ff. 84r-174v. Way collated the Promptorium under siglum H.
entities.label: British Library, Add MS 37789

access.accessMethod: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog
Date-bounded review of the current BL record and targeted manuscript-digitization searches
access.format: No whole-object public digital surrogate located
notes: The current record exposes no Digitised Content.
Way's printed image of f. 73v is only one page and is separately modeled as partial.
Any complete request should include modern Way material ff. 1-3, all medieval leaves, covers, endleaves, and blanks; no order was placed.

entities.description: Imperfect fifteenth-century parchment Promptorium acquired after the first two Way parts; Way's letter and notes now occupy modern ff. 1-3, and the medieval text survives at ff. 5r-110v.
entities.label: British Library, Add MS 22556

physicalDescription.extent: Promptorium ff. 5r-110v, preceded by modern Way material at ff. 1-3

bibliographic.citation: Promptorius puerorum, printed in London by Richard Pynson at the expense of Frederick Egmondt and Petrus post Pascha, 15 May 1499 (GW 10483; ISTC ip01011000).
bibliographic.publisher: Richard Pynson for Frederick Egmondt and Petrus post Pascha
bibliographic.title: Promptorius puerorum

entities.description: The first printed version, extensively collated by Way under siglum P; the edition's prologue and separated nomina/verba arrangement represent a version distinct from any one surviving manuscript.
entities.label: Richard Pynson, Promptorius puerorum (1499)

physicalDescription.extent: 116 unnumbered leaves in the ideal copy, with t4 blank

access.accessMethod: Penn Colenda catalog viewer or IIIF Presentation manifest; complete exposed physical object cached as a 238-image IIIF bundle
access.entityLabel: Penn Libraries complete digital facsimile of Inc P-1011 Kislak copy
access.format: 238-canvas IIIF Presentation 2 object and JPEG images

localCopies.label: Complete UPenn Kislak scan of Pynson 1499 at non-upscaled 1600-pixel width
notes: The ZIP includes the source IIIF manifest, normalized inventory, and all 238 exposed canvas images.

Completeness is relative to the exposed physical Kislak copy. The ideal edition has 116 leaves including blank t4, but this copy lacks t4; covers, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, and every surviving printed surface are included.
Images were requested at the IIIF 1600-pixel-wide profile without upscaling.

workPortion.label: Pynson 1499 Promptorium text collated by Albert Way
notes: Canvas 8 is the prologue on a1v; canvas 9 begins the lexicon on a2r; canvas 231 has the colophon on t3r; canvas 232 has Pynson's device on t3v.

next_steps: Ask the British Library to assess complete-object photography of Harley MS 221, Harley MS 2274, Add MS 37789, and Add MS 22556, including covers, bindings, endleaves, blanks, adjacent texts, and every folio rather than only the Promptorium portions.

Ask King's College Library and Winchester Cathedral Library to confirm current shelfmarks/catalog records, image availability, whole-codex reproduction feasibility, fees, and reuse terms for MS 8 and MS 15.
Record delivered-image rights and contract terms before caching any requested files.
notes: The two cached Way plates are expressly partial and do not satisfy this question.
Generic IIIF/Mirador viewer chrome without a manifest or canvases is not evidence that a manuscript has been digitized.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable whole-object digital facsimiles be obtained for all six manuscript sources used by Way?

next_steps: Compare the preambles, additions, omissions, source lists, and nomina/verba sequences across all extant whole and fragmentary witnesses rather than treating Way's collated text as the original work.
Test Considine's model that Add MS 37789 represents an earlier stage and Harley/Winchester later accretions against complete witness images and diplomatic transcriptions.
Integrate the Emmanuel College pastedown fragments and other witnesses not used by Way into a tradition-level census.
openQuestions.question: Does the 1440 date record the original compilation or only the expanded redaction represented by Harley MS 221, and how are the nine extant witnesses related stemmatically?

next_steps: Ask U-M Library for the item-level keying specification, source-copy identifier, transformation history, proofreading log, and rationale for the encoded Way boundaries.
Compare any supplied production record with TEMPHEAD and the deterministic XML-to-print boundary analysis.
openQuestions.question: What item-specific U-M production record identifies the exact source copy, transcription/keying vendor or staff workflow, and selection rationale for CME00028?

next_steps: Crosswalk Way's Grenville 1508 copy and former Heber/Madden 1516 copy through current British Library and other institutional provenance records.
Recheck ESTC, USTC, EEBO, institutional catalogs, and newly exposed IIIF services for complete copy-specific surrogates, distinguishing subscription access from reusable public images.
If a public copy is found, cache the complete physical volume including binding and adjacent works and record exact Way-work boundaries.
openQuestions.question: What are the current shelfmarks and complete digital-surrogate states of the exact Julian Notary 1508 and Wynkyn de Worde 1516 copies collated by Way?

reviewNotes: The abstract Promptorium is cataloged in the paired work-metadata record; the lineage primary subject is the concrete repository artifact, following the repository model.
Way's H is crosswalked to current Add MS 37789 / former Phillipps 8306 and Heber 1360. The adjacent Medulla grammaticae is explicitly excluded from the Promptorium witness scope.
The three LG and 825 L nodes in the XML are quotations inside Way's editorial apparatus, so they do not change the medieval work's prose form.
No external reproduction request was sent. All whole-manuscript negatives are dated research results, not claims that no private, subscription, or future surrogate exists.

notes: The ZIP includes the source IIIF manifest, normalized inventory, and all 238 exposed canvas images.

Completeness is relative to the exposed physical Kislak copy. The ideal edition has 116 leaves including blank t4, but this copy lacks t4; covers, pastedowns, endleaves, blanks, and every surviving printed surface are included.
Images were requested at the IIIF 1600-pixel-wide profile without upscaling.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a copy of the University of Michigan CME digital text, manually keyed and reviewed from selected portions of Albert Way's three-part 1843-1865 Camden Society edition. Way used British Library Harley MS 221 as his base text and collated five other manuscript sources plus the Pynson 1499, Julian Notary 1508, and Wynkyn de Worde 1516 printings. This record keeps the abstract medieval work, its expanding manuscript redactions, physical witnesses, early print versions, Way's editorial edition, U-M's encoding, the repository artifact, print scans, and the two printed manuscript plates distinct.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Dominican compiler of King's Lynn; Richard Fraunces tentatively proposed; older Geoffrey the Grammarian attribution disputed
catalogSummary.date: Dated 1440 in the Harley redaction; first printed 15 May 1499
catalogSummary.region: King's Lynn and Norfolk, England; the compiler explicitly followed Norfolk usage, while the Harley base witness is probably not East Anglian
catalogSummary.title: Promptorium parvulorum

catalogingSubject.label: Promptorium parvulorum
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous bilingual Middle English-Latin lexicon associated with a Dominican friar of King's Lynn, Norfolk, and dated 1440 in the Harley redaction, including its Latin preamble and alphabetic word-list. The record does not identify this abstract medieval work with Harley MS 221 or any other physical witness, the early printed versions, Albert Way's three-part 1843-1865 scholarly edition and commentary, A. L. Mayhew's 1908 edition, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML.

contentParts.label: Preambulum
contentParts.summary: Latin explanation of the work's educational purpose, sources, alphabetic organization, Norfolk usage, correction request, compiler status, and 1440 date.

contentParts.label: Alphabetic lexicon
notes: The 11,797 count includes direct encoded ITEM entries under the letter divisions and excludes nested editorial lists and the 37 additional-notes ITEM nodes.
The three LG and 825 L nodes occur in Way's quoted illustrative material and do not make the medieval lexicon mixed-form.
contentParts.summary: Middle English headwords and explanatory phrases with Latin equivalents, serialized by Way and the XML in 23 letter divisions from A through yogh.

assertion.notes: The explicit following the preamble dates the collection of the vocabulary to Anno Domini 1440. Considine cautions that this may date the expanded Harley redaction or may preserve knowledge of the original compilation date; it is therefore retained as the conventional work date with qualified confidence.

extent.description: Encoding metrics: one preamble plus 23 direct alphabetic DIV3 sections; 11,797 direct DIV3/LIST/ITEM lexicon entries. The XML also contains 1,949 notes, three verse groups and 825 L nodes inside Way's editorial quotations and apparatus; those lines are not intrinsic verse structure of the medieval lexicon.

formStatements.description: A prose preamble followed by alphabetically arranged lexical entries; no intrinsic verse section is modeled.

assertion.notes: The preamble says the work was compiled for young clerics seeking Latin vocabulary.

scope.description: English headwords, synonyms, and explanatory phrases

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The work is genuinely bilingual: Latin is structural content, not merely occasional quotation.

scope.description: Latin preamble, equivalents, grammatical labels, citations, and source list

notes: The exact sources named after the medieval preamble include the Catholicon, Campus florum, Dictionarius and works associated with Huguccio, William Brito, John of Garland, Robert Kilwardby, Alexander Neckam and others; uncertain modern identifications are not converted into direct lineage entities.

Way's printed title-page claim that the work is by Frater Galfridus is preserved as edition-level attribution, not treated as a medieval self-identification; current scholarship instead treats Richard Fraunces as a plausible but unverified name.
The XML's TEXT/@LANG value 'enm lat' correctly signals a bilingual work but does not describe Way's surrounding modern English commentary as part of the abstract medieval work.

next_steps: Trace the 1499 copy formerly owned by Thomas Ward of Longbridge in which Thomas Hearne reported the Richard Fraunces annotation and inspect the inscription if the copy survives.

Compare the name and anchorite description with King's Lynn Dominican, episcopal, testamentary, and enclosure records; separately reassess the Bale-derived Geoffrey annotation in Cambridge University Library Inc.3.J.3.6[3619].
openQuestions.question: Can the compiler's personal identity be established by directly verifying the lost-or-unlocated annotation naming Richard Fraunces or through independent Dominican records?

assertion.notes: The compiler identifies himself as a Dominican recluse of Lynn; this supports the compilation association without proving a specific building or anchorage.
The preamble says the compiler followed the Norfolk manner of speaking learned from infancy. The British Library separately cautions that Harley MS 221 itself is probably not East Anglian.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Dominican compiler of King's Lynn

assertion.notes: The work self-identifies its compiler as a Dominican friar or preacher and recluse of Lynn, but does not supply a personal name.

This records the historical attribution rather than endorsing it. No medieval Promptorium witness names Geoffrey; Considine reconstructs the attribution as a mistake arising from Bale's use of printed grammatical Sammelbande, and the distinct Medulla grammaticae has also been confused with this work.

Considine treats Richard Fraunces as a plausible identification, not a certainty: Hearne's reported annotation supplies the name and the independently corroborated anchorite detail, but no extant medieval witness names its compiler.

scope.description: Immediate 1843-1865 source edition and its commentary
Harley MS 221 base text with readings and additions from five other manuscripts and three early printed editions

summaries.value: Dated 1440 in the Harley redaction and designed for learners who needed ready access to Latin vocabulary, the Promptorium parvulorum pairs Middle English headwords and explanatory phrases with Latin equivalents and grammatical information. Its compiler identifies himself only as a Dominican friar or preacher and recluse of Lynn who followed Norfolk speech. The conventional name Geoffrey the Grammarian is a later, disputed attribution; Richard Fraunces is a plausible but unverified alternative. The medieval arrangement separates nomina from verba within alphabetic sections; Way's immediate source edition instead merges them into one alphabetic sequence and adds extensive commentary.

The repository XML has two 1843/1853 volume-title divisions, the medieval preamble, 23 letter divisions containing 11,797 direct lexicon ITEM nodes, Way's local notes and early-print colophons through printed p. 540, and four pages of additional notes/corrections. It omits the 1865 title, Advertisement, Way's long Preface and Appendix, the orthographic index on pp. 541-550, the subject index on pp. 551-560, and binder matter. These are encoding metrics and selection facts, not a canonical medieval headword count.

assertion.notes: The title is stated at the end of the encoded preamble and is the established short title in the current British Library record.
Way records this form from King's College MS 8.
The 1499 Pynson prologue uses this form.
Way records this title from Wynkyn de Worde's editions.
This historical printed title must not be confused with the distinct Latin-English work also called Medulla grammaticae.

scope.description: Title applied to the Promptorium in the 1499 colophon and later printed editions
Shortened form of the title of Albert Way's immediate source edition

Its compiler identifies himself only as a Dominican friar or preacher and recluse of Lynn who followed Norfolk speech. The conventional name Geoffrey the Grammarian is a later, disputed attribution; Richard Fraunces is a plausible but unverified alternative. The medieval arrangement separates nomina from verba within alphabetic sections; Way's immediate source edition instead merges them into one alphabetic sequence and adds extensive commentary.
